sentences.text: We're coming.
He's just signing.
[INAUDIBLE] now?
No.
Yes, my name is William Basch.
I was born in a small village in the Carpathian Mountains.
When I was born, it was Czechoslovakia.
And it was the most eastern side, eastern tip of Czechoslovakia, small, Ruthenian village.
At that time it was called Sasovo.
It has changed since then.
That territory belonged to Hungary-Austria prior to 1914.
In 1914, when Czechoslovakia was created, it was created out of three territories, Bohemia, Slovakia, and Carpathia.
So 1918 to 1938, it became Czechoslovakia.
In 1938, because the Hungarians had an allied agreement sort of alliance with Hitler, he allowed them to rip Czechoslovakia part and has given back that territory to the Hungarians.
So in 1938, it became under the supervision of the Hungarian government.
No, what they did, the Russians, they annexed the territory.
Instead of being just an occupied satellite, they annexed it to the Ukraine because that gave them an access, a direct route from Moscow through the Ukraine to Germany without crossing any other.
Territorial-wise, they needed that area.
So  it's annexed to the Ukraine, and it has never been returned, neither to Hungary, neither to Czechoslovakia.
It is part of the Ukraine.
June 4.
Basically two.
There were-- we referred to them as the Ruthenians, or Russian peasantry.
It was a farming village.
And the population was mostly Greek Orthodox-- oddly, but that's how it was.
There were Greek Orthodox, approximately 3,000 Gentile people and approximately 35 to 40 Jewish families living in that village.
That was the whole village, about 3,000-plus.
In our home, of course, I had my father, mother.
Then I had an older brother.
His name was Arthur, my oldest brother.
And after-- my father's name was Martin, Martin Basch.
My mother's name was Gizella, or Gizella Hartstein Basch.
My oldest brother was Arthur Basch.
Then came Ted Basch.
Then came the first-- I'm sorry.
I came third.
I was the third boy in a row, William Basch.
And then came a girl called Edith Basch.
And then came a younger sister, called Pearl.
Or in Hungarian, we named her [HUNGARIAN].
But that would be called here Pearl.
So that consisted of the immediate family.
However, my mother became an orphan at age of four.
She lost both her father and her mother.
And she was adopted by her uncle, Hartstein, who lived in that village.
So I have never known my original grandparents.
My grandparents were my mother's uncle and aunt who adopted my mother because she was an orphan at four.
So then we had assistants.
We had the gypsies.
We had a gypsy lady who was with the family, with my grandparents who had an illegitimate child.
And she also lived in our house, a gypsy mother and gypsy daughter.
But they were just maintaining the household.
So they worked for the--
They worked.
They worked for the family.
They almost belonged to the family.
They have never known any other place but the Hartstein and subsequently, the Basch family.
So they were like family.
No, I cannot remember the gypsy lady's name.
Well, in that village, we had the largest what's called community store.
Community store at that time consisted of general grocery store, liquor store, lumber yard, [INAUDIBLE], whatever.
Since child-- if a child was born, they came to our store to buy whatever they needed for that little child to build a crib.
It was-- remember, it was 50 years ago in a remote village.
So everything was from our place.
When the child grew up, got married, they bought the fabric to sew up the wedding gown in our store.
And as the same person got older, even to the point where the lumber for the casket was also bought from us.
So we were sort of a central point in that village.
And anything that was needed by the population, that's where they had to get it.
We even-- we had the only-- what would you call it-- a tavern, the only tavern.
But we grew our own grape.
We had a vineyard.
So we grew our own grape, pressed our own grape, made our own wine.
And that serviced the population, the people, those that wanted to drink.
So we had that.
And then we had quite a bit of land-- approximately 10 families that lived off our land.
It was sharecropping.
My father was a businessman.
He didn't have time to attend to the land.
So he gave it out to different families.
At harvest time, we shared.
So we eventually sold the grain, also, to the people that-- especially the Jewish people, that did not have land.
They bought the grain to bake the bread.
In that area, under those circumstances, they were very well-off, well-off, respected.
And they were sort of a strength and a key in that little village.
In today's standard where we live, it would be different.
But at that time, yes, they were definitely sort of a powerhouse of that village.
It was a central, focal point for many things.
Culturally, everything was happening around our house.
And also, when there was a need, most people were illiterate, and my father acted as an interpreter.
He acted as the-- we didn't have an official government.
But if it were the closest thing to what my father did, he was sort of like the mayor of the town.
All the official documents, he signed.
All the official papers came through him.
And Mr.
Basch was looked upon as the man who knows all the answers.
So yes, we were an important family.
The village-- most people spoke a Ukrainian dialect, Ruthenian Ukrainian dialect.
However, there were some that, not so much the children, as the parents, spoke Hungarian.
Because remember, they were older, and they were from Hungary-Austria time, so they still spoke Hungarians, few.
Most Jews spoke Yiddish, Ukrainian, and Hungarian.
I have had to learn, when I was born, Hungarian to communicate with the maid.
Then I immediately, at age of three, thee and a half, we started going to cheder, which was the name for the Hebrew school, studying the Yiddish, or the Hebrew, the Torah.
And there I had to learn the Hebrew and the Yiddish.
And then at home, we also spoke Yiddish.
Then I went to public school.
I had to speak the Ukrainian, which was a Ruthenian language.
So that made it immediately three languages.
Then later on, it was compulsory to learn at least the basics of Czech because Czechoslovakia was the country that controlled it.
So simultaneously, by the age of six or so, I spoke Hungarian, Yiddish, Ivrit, really, more than Hebrew, Ukrainian, and some Czech.
They spoke all those languages.
However, at home, we used to-- we used either Yiddish or Hungarian.
We did not use the Ukrainian language because we never felt comfortable.
It was not our language.
It was something we had to do.
No.
No, the Jewish familiar were-- at least, they were always literate in the Hebrew and in the Yiddish.
Very few were illiterate in the what was accepted as the governmental language, the Ukrainian language, very few.
But all were literate, at least in Hebrew.
But there were many, many peasant kids that didn't ever bother sending their kids to school at all.
And it was not uncommon to find complete families that were illiterate.
It was tradition, tradition that it was unheard of of a Jewish kid not to start going to Hebrew school at age of four.
It was unheard of of a Jewish kid disobeying the governmental orders and not to go to school, to public school, no matter how hard it was.
It was a given that you go to school.
You attend school.
And eventually, everyone was literate.
That was a way of life.
Jewish people just did not disobey government orders.
Yes, however, the bar mitzvahs were not as we know them today.
They were very traditional.
And it consisted of mostly the prayers to be done in the little shul that we had and possibly a small, little lunch.
It was not an elaborate bar mitzvah as today.
This is all new.
This is a new invention.
Oh, you had to read from the Torah.
You had to be fluent in Ivrit, definitely.
You had to do that.
That was the concentration, knowing the Ivrit, knowing the Torah.
That meant to be bar mitzvahed, that you have understood that.
You have understood why you were bar mitzvah.
The party was very small.
Yes.
Yes, in every respect, but we were not solely just Hasidic man here.
My father was a-- wore a very modern outfit, suit and tie, and always a little hat, but not skull cap.
He didn't wear this wide-brim hat.
He was not a fanatic in any way, very learned about in the Jewish faith, in the Jewish way, and also, he was self-thought in worldly matters.
So he, he was not the typical Jew as you would imagine in a small shtetl.
He was a very modern businessman, but very religious.
Yes, he observed the religion.
We had kosher in the house.
It was unheard of not to.
And we observed the Shabbat to the tradition.
And we were part of the Jewish community, and very strong.
And he was also leader of the Jewish community in that village.
So we were very Jewish, but not fanatically religious, no.
That, we were not.
What about Jewish institutions or synagogues in the town.
Were there any?
Well, since it was a small town, we had only a small little shul.
It was mostly built out of clay bricks.
And it consisted of two rooms because remember, there were only 30 families.
So all we had to do is pass-- at the maximum have 50, 60 people.
So it was not a large.
We could not support a rabbi.
However, what we supported at that time-- and I wish I knew the English name of it-- it was called a shochet.
Shochet is a person who performs the killing of the animals.
Now, remember, we did not have butcher shops.
Everybody had their chicken.
Everybody had their geese.
Some had their cows.
And there was one butcher, and that's where [INAUDIBLE].
So we had to come to this man to perform the proper religious way, the killing, which was a merciful type of killing.
And it was very common Friday where Friday morning, I would take down either the geese or the chicken, and take it to shochet.
And he would perform.
He would cut the throat and test the lungs, blow up the lungs, see for any damages.
Because in the Jewish tradition, if you pull the lungs out, and especially from a geese, and there are lungs are spattered, they're trayf.
There's a little humorous story to this.
My father-- I was always the-- among the three boys, I was considered to be the most religious one and destined to be a rabbi.
So my father always told me, he says, son, when you go to the shochet and he performs the test, one thing never do.
Never ask, is it kosher?
He may say no.
But if you don't ask, and you come home, and he didn't say anything, it's kosher.
[LAUGHTER]
Because there was a tradition at that time, and when you have three sons-- and this goes back long, long in the bible-- where one son gets the inheritance of the family.
And the other son becomes a tradesman, a professional man or a tradesman.
And the third son studies Torah.
And it's the responsibility of those two sons to support the third son.
While my father was not that religious that he would follow that tradition, but more or less, he did choose one son who would carry on the religious tradition, or possibly become a rabbi or whatever.
This was a wish or dream.
And the one that was destined to be that, it's me because my oldest brother was very bright, very intelligent.
At 13, he was speaking English, self-taught.
My second brother was very handy, [?
Tibor, ?] or Ted, who is alive.
He was very handy with his hands.
He made a fine mechanic He was destined to be the mechanic, or the professional.
And I was destined to be the rabbi.
But things did not turn out that way at all.
Thank God.
Thank God because while I have the greatest admiration for the rabbis-- I have great compassion of how hard it is to survive as a rabbi, because rabbi has to please everybody.
And he has no opportunity of ever becoming a great businessman.
And it's a dedicated life.
And it's not an easy life.
So I am glad that I've not become a rabbi because then, I would be faced with the same hardship as so many rabbis do.
Unless you are one of the great ones from one of the great synagogues, most of them dedicate themselves and they don't get back what they put into the community.
Do you think after the Holocaust, you actually could have become a rabbi after everything that happened?
Never had desire for it.
I never had a desire for it.
So I--
Very much.
Yes.
Without any question.
I keep all the Jewish traditions.
I am not very religious.
I was more religious while my wife, who passed on-- when we had our home, and we raised our children, we kept kosher.
We kept the tradition.
We went to temple.
But losing a wife and being alone with obligations of raising three children-- well, they were not really small, but they were not independent yet either.
I have not been able to maintain the religion as I would like it.
So I have chosen the middle of the road conservatism.
And I observe the conservative doctrine.
[SIGHS] It is a very-- that is-- that's difficult question because I did not have, after the survival, time to think of faith immediately after the survival.
It was surviving.
Not only being freed by the Americans, not only getting out of the camps, but surviving thereafter was very, very difficult.
And whether my beliefs in the Jewish faith was shaken-- not as a Jew, but as a religion I have questioned myself many times.
If it's a just God, how he could he stand by?
And I'd like to believe that he's a merciful and he's a just God.
Yes, it was very painful for me to accept God in the way I have accepted him, unquestionably when I was a child.
I didn't question it.
I believed in it the way I was taught.

After the Holocaust, I did not alienate the belief in God, but it was a very, very torturous, very difficult to continually believe in the same faith, with the same faith, with the same impact in God.
Eventually, I have compromised.
I still believe in God, but I'm not as dedicated as a Jew as I was.
I'm dedicated to Judaism, but not the religion as much as someone would want me to.
Well, I participate as a Jew in everything that is Jewish.
Judaism is-- the religious-- I separate the religion from the morality and the spirituality of being a Jew.
I live a Jewish life.
I attend all Jewish activities.
I get myself involved in Jewish charitable work.
I believe in the promotion of the Jewish faith, raising children to understand the Jewish faith.
But I'm not dedicated to the temple.
I attend temple two, three times a year.
But I am-- what I have been for years, a member of the Board of Directors at Sinai Temple, which is the largest conservative synagogue.
As time goes on, I find that I can be a very good Jew without the need of being in a temple.
I'm strong enough to raise my spirits as a Jew.
So this moment, I cannot-- possibly later, I'll think, why didn't I say something brighter?
[LAUGHS] We all do that.
But at this moment, all I can say is that I'm proud to be a Jew.
And I am a Jew with my whole heart and soul.
I do everything I can that my grandchildren should understand what a Jew is.
And I will remain a Jew the rest of my days, but not a dedicated religious Jew.
Why is it important for you to be a good Jew?
Maybe you've already answered that.
But why do you think it's important?
To begin with, what's the alternative?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What is the alternative?
Not to belong-- being born a Jew, and not to belong to society, and become an individual, and if you become individual, you alienate yourself from a structure that I was born in, raised with.
My body and soul has always been surrounded with the Judaic causes.
I wouldn't know how not to be.
It is natural to me as breathing.
And not to do it, it would be as unnaturally as stop breathing.
Yes.
It was both bittersweet.
We had a very wonderful tradition at home, temple, as long as we did not have to go out to the outside, out of doors.
When we did go out of doors, we felt anti-Semitism all around us.
We felt that those little Gentile kids still believed that we have killed Jesus.
They still believed that on Easter, we kill a child and drink the child's blood to the point where many Jewish people on Easter didn't even show their-- didn't get out of the house.
They believed in the blood sacrifice, you know, the blood libel.
Therefore, as little children not understanding what they are talking about knew that a Jew is to be hated.
He killed our Lord.
Well, when you attend public school, and at that time, religion was-- in this country, we have separation of religion from, from, from government--
--yeah, religion from state.
It was not the case there.
For example, twice a month, the priest came and taught the Gentile kids the religion, the Gentile religion.
Well, we have had the privilege of stepping out of that room, and we went to cheder for those few hours.
But we did not sit in the classroom or get classroom time.
So immediately, Jews stepped out because no one would sit in and listen to the priest.
It was an awesome feeling because name-calling, you know, stone-throwing from the little kids and--
[UKRAINIAN] go back to Palestine.
You don't belong here.
And, you know, you felt it every moment in every day.
You were-- you were needed, but not necessarily loved.
I cannot do it properly.
But [UKRAINIAN] is Jew in Ukrainian.
Go to Palestine.
You belong to Palestine.
Go to Jerusalem.
They didn't even know what Palestine.
They didn't know what Jerusalem meant.
They heard it from their parents.
Kids repeated it.
And that was the feeling that existed in most homes.
There was no love for the Jew at all.
The Jew was needed because the Jew was the carpenter.
The Jew was the shoemaker.
The Jew was a tradesman.
The Jew had the store.
The Jew had the land.
So they were needed, maybe even somewhat respected, but never loved.
And that's-- those were the circumstances under which we lived.
Only in the larger cities.
In the small cities, they were the same.
They were just as bad.
Remember that the Ukrainians that lived in our village were not the ones that lived in the deep Ukraine.
They were Ruthenian.
They were an off root from the Ukrainians.
However, in small villages, it was all over, more or less the same thing.
It was much worse in Poland, of course.
Jews were much more hated.
And it was easier to pick at them because they were isolated.
They lived in more in a shtetl type of atmosphere among themselves for self-protection.
We didn't.
We lived-- we lived everywhere in that village.
There was a Jewish house.
There were 20 Gentiles.
There's another Jewish house.
So we did not-- we were not isolated, whereby they could have a pogrom or anything like that.
That did not exist.
And as far as the small villages, there were no better one than another, whatever country it was in.
But in larger cities, there were more educated people, so it was not as noticeable.
You mentioned that it was worse for the Polish Jews.
Is that something you knew then, or is that something you know now?
We knew then, but not to do the degree that we know now.
Well, we heard-- we were taken over by the Hungarians in 1938.
And the Germans attacked Poland.
They had to go through Carpethia through Galitzia to get into Poland.
And it was 1939.
In 1940, rumors started coming through that they're gathering up the Jews.
We didn't know exactly how bad it is.
And at least we children didn't know.
I believe my father and mother knew.
They knew much more.
But they sheltered us.
They sheltered us as much as the could from the horrors that they knew that exists.
So as time went on, more and more filtered down from our parents to us.
Because eventually, they reasoned with us, rationalized and explained to us what we should know.
But were--
